speaker: interviewer
text: Tape seven of the interview with Lillian Bielsky-Bell, December 1st, 1996. You said that you lived- you were in Romania for six months.

speaker: interviewee
text: From Hungary, we came to Czechoslovakia, and then we came to Romania-- to Hungary, and from Hungary we went to Romania. But this was already by directing complete, you know, supervising by the Shlichim from Israel.

speaker: interviewer
text: And--

speaker: interviewee
text: They helped us already with a few dollars, and they helped us, you know, to exist, and they gave us-- gave us instruction how to go about.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you live in Romania?

speaker: interviewee
text: They gave us places.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where? In what city?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Romania? In Romania, in Bucharest. We were in a kibbutz. I was in Romania in the seventh month of pregnancy. They used to put me on trains like a ball, or I fell off the train, or I-- I-- [LAUGHS] I rolled like a ball. It wasn't-- it was right after the war. Nothing was normal. It was military trains. It wasn't-- it wasn't-- nothing was set yet.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of a kibbutz was it that you were living in?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was a kibbutz only for these survivors that came from all over.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it by a particular Zionist group?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I don't think it was a political group. It was just for survivors to give them a stop, like a hotel, to sleep, to eat, and to be free to rest up.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the names of any of the Shlichim who were helping you?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I didn't deal with them. My husband dealt with them. He went for the meetings. He went [INAUDIBLE]. I was in the seventh month pregnancy, had a very hard time. I was hospitalized for a few weeks in the hospital because I had to do with the kidneys. And this is-- everything strange. The language was strange to me. The whole thing in Romania was after war too, don't forget. So it wasn't easy. And then I had a normal birth.

speaker: interviewer
text: And was that in the hospital?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was in a hospital, and I came back to the kibbutz. And I didn't have enough milk. The baby wasn't eating. And the baby was four months. They told me to [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] spit up. And he had a lot of-- he had a bad diarrhea. And they said my milk is no good. It's too much poison in the milk, you know, from nerves, and-- and maybe I wasn't-- my-- after the war, maybe I didn't have all the vitamins. Maybe I-- I was weak. I was weak. So they told me I should hire somebody.

speaker: interviewer
text: To nurse the baby?

speaker: interviewee
text: To nurse the baby. And they-- somebody recommended me a woman. It was advertised in the paper. I didn't know that her baby was-- she had a baby, and had 11 months baby, or even older. And she was nursing my baby. My baby got sicker and got a bigger diarrhea, finished with pneumonia. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] For me, it was a terrible thing after the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was that a boy or a girl?

speaker: interviewee
text: A boy. Was named after my father.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you do any-- after that, after you recovered, did you and Tuvia-- did you do any work in the kibbutz?

speaker: interviewee
text: That wasn't a kibbutz for work. I told you it was a kibbutz, called a kibbutz, because it was a ghetto place, but actually it was hotel to station the people that were on their way to Israel.

speaker: interviewer
text: And-- and who supported you? Who gave you the money?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Shlichim.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did you finally leave and go to Israel?

speaker: interviewee
text: When the time came, and the-- the Transylvania boat was ready for us.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was that the name of the boat?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: The Transylvania?

speaker: interviewee
text: Right. And and whoever, I guess they were waiting for the British certificates to get legal to the country, because that was the British mandate when we got to Israel.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was 1945, Sukkah's time. It was September or October.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. And when you came to the-- the trip on the boat was--

speaker: interviewee
text: Was terrible. I was dead. I just didn't show up, not once, on-- with all the people. I was so sick. It was-- you know, it wasn't today's boats. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I was very sick. I couldn't wait to get off the boat, because I thought I'll never get to Israel. And it was very emotional. [INAUDIBLE] they didn't let in the boat to Haifa. My sister-in-law had-- she find out on the boat-- we knew, but she didn't know-- that she had two brothers in Israel, and one of her brothers in [INAUDIBLE] Kibbutz was shot by another friend, by another-- by another kibbutznik. And it was a young guy. She didn't know. She got up one morning. She said, you know, I had such a bad dream that this one, I-- my brother was shot. I said, [NON-ENGLISH]. We knew. We knew already. And then her other brother from kibbutz, [? Don, ?] came to meet us at the airport and they didn't let us off the-- let us off the boat because it was a holiday. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] So after that we went to the kibbutz.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which kibbutz?

speaker: interviewee
text: [? Don. ?] Kibbutz Dan, [NON-ENGLISH]. And there we stayed two weeks. And people thought that we came from the other world, which it was. I was-- I-- that's when I found out that I'm pregnant again. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And that's when I had my daughter in Israel.

speaker: interviewer
text: And after the-- after those two weeks in the kibbutz, where did you go?

speaker: interviewee
text: Kibbutz, and so they looked at us. My husband had, every single night, he had for three, four hours, speeches there in the dining room. And the kibbutznik kids used to listen, and they believed and they didn't-- they lived-- I remember, when we left Israel, my-- my daughter and my sister-in-law's son-- difference in a half a year in age-- they went for a walk in kibbutz, and they went over the-- the border. And it was a [NON-ENGLISH]. It was alarm in the kibbutz, because two kids went over the border from-- so close we were at the border there, Kibbutz [? Don. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: So then it was very hard. Very hard. They couldn't get-- they couldn't get any work. And then-- but because of their name and of their survival, and then, slowly, [NON-ENGLISH] started to come to partisans, and they started to tell the story where they were sur-- where they survived, and how it was, and who was their commander.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he really got a tremendous name there. And he wrote a book in '45, [NON-ENGLISH], [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] [NON-ENGLISH]. No, [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: [NON-ENGLISH] .

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. And he wrote with his brother this book. And when he finished, he was the first one to be killed in-- we came from war to war. Then the British left, and the Haganah-- the [NON-ENGLISH] and the Haganah, everybody took over on their own. Then they arrested Sharett and Golda Meir and they put them in jail, and that's when Sharett read the book of [NON-ENGLISH], and he came to look for my husband. And my daughter was about seven months old. No, four months old. And we lived in Holon. Holon is sand, and sand, and again sand. And in bed was sand, and on the table and in plate, all over. And all of a sudden-- and this was already the times weren't too quiet-- because the Arab-- the war with the Arabs started for independence of Israel. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] And in Holon was no sidewalks, no nothing. It was just, you know, the-- the old Holon was a little more built. But where the [NON-ENGLISH] was ours, wherever it was given by the government to us, it was terrible.

speaker: interviewee
text: So all of a sudden, a big, long limousine stops near my window. I never-- I never looked in this side. I was happy that no one would come in, because I just washed the floor. It was pretty hot. It was in Israel. And my daughter was-- wanted to go to sleep. So I said-- what I look in the-- I didn't even look. All of a sudden, a knock on the door. Knock on the door. I opened the door. Two men, very well-dressed, chic. Two behind them with guns. I said, what the heck is going on? Are you going to arrest me, or again kill me? And they said, may we come in? In a way, I wanted to say no. You'll bring me, again, sand. But I had no choice. My chairs were on the table. One room with the child, with everything. I shared the kitchen with my sister-in-law, and the-- the bathroom. But I had no choice. They came in. He goes into the-- the crib, and he takes the baby out. I said, that's a chutzpah. How could you do that? But I didn't say a boo. And he played with her. He says, where's Tuvia? I said, Tuvia's looking for a job. He's never home until he would find it. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: But it hel-- it had-- it will have to be like this. So he says to me, I am here visiting my sister. I would love-- I'll send a limousine for Tuvia, and tell him that Moshe Sharett was sitting in jail and he read his book, and he wants to see him in person. I almost fell on the floor. Not to sit down, not to-- not to maybe want something. But give him my regards for Moshe Sharett, as-- at that time he was Shertok, not Sharett.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was his position in the government at that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was a time-- he was a-- a minister-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: Foreign minister.

speaker: interviewee
text: Foreign minister, and then he was minister. I almost [INAUDIBLE] oh my God. Look at him. He said, Tuvia has a wife with a behavior, with intelligence. Didn't even ask to sit down. He put the baby back, and said please, do me a favor. Tell him that I'm looking for him, and I'll send later the limousine for him. My sister-in-law was at the neighbor. I ran like crazy. I said Sonia, you'll probably think that I'm crazy, but Moshe Shertok was here. She says-- she says, you don't know what you're talking about. You are dreaming. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: I said to her, OK, if I'm dreaming, why didn't you stay home and see it by yourself? Sure enough, at night, [INAUDIBLE] was in camp. Tuvia came home. He took him away and he was a great help to us. He was very involved. He was very devoted to us. What kind of work did Tuvia do in Israel?

speaker: interviewee
text: Tuvia went straight to the army. That time, they fought with sticks and-- and pens. But he was very ambitious, and he was always on guard. And he-- and they trust him a whole group again. And Holon was right a neighbor from the Arab little-- from Ram-- Ramallah and from Lod and from all those places. Holon is in this direction. And-- but we had a grocery in the beginning. Then they took him to the army. So I was alone, and I had to give up because the baby was very small, 11 months. And I got already notice that he-- that he's-- he disappeared, and they don't know where he is. And-- and this was in Kippur, and I walked to Tel Aviv from Holon. And I went to the-- to the main office, you know, from the-- where they sent the people out, and they know more or less, at least, who is [NON-ENGLISH] or what-- how-- missing. Missing.

speaker: interviewee
text: And how did it happen? I was standing after I closed the store, and it was already Kippur, and I know that those holidays were never separated. No matter where he was, and no matter what he did, we always were together, because we believed in it. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: And I'm going-- I'm taking the baby and I'm going out. We didn't have any advertisements or special radios or television. So on the [NON-ENGLISH], on the--

speaker: interviewer
text: Posts.

speaker: interviewee
text: Posts, there was all the news of what's going on around. And I'm standing there reading the news, and near me, standing a Bulgarian Jew that he spoke a very poor Hebrew. And he says to the other, you know, how do you like-- Bielsky's dead too already.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I'm standing with the baby, so the other one is asking, which one? There's two brothers. He says-- he described him, and it's Tuvia. I came home. I said-- then I think to myself, they don't know the language. Maybe they-- they just talking rumors [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewee
text: I went home. I light the candles. I put the [NON-ENGLISH] candle. And I'm standing, waiting. Nobody shows up. I said, that's impossible. I start to believe. And I went-- I went to my neighbor's. I was crying. I said, I have to leave the baby with you. I must go tomorrow morning to Tel Aviv and find out at least where he was killed, where is he missing. Sure enough, I went to Tel Aviv. So one friend said, you're not going yourself. It's [? Yom ?] Kippur. I don't know what's on the roads. I'll go with you. And his wife kept the baby, and I-- and I went with him. Came there and I said-- and they starting to tell me already. Now, you know you went through a war, and the whole [NON-ENGLISH]. It's not so easy and you have to get used to the idea. I said, no ideas you can give me. All the ideas I know, and everything I've got used to already. And I live. I'm here. But tell me, where is my husband missing. They said, I'm sorry, we cannot tell you. Go home. If we'll know something. I said, I have a little baby home. And I have no sources of living. Tell me where he is. So they said, we cannot tell you because we don't know. He's missing. He was in a truck. And this was the last thing for the-- with the Egyptian army that they fought in Negev. He saw that the truck is being all the time attacked, so he fell out from the trunk, from the trunk-- from the truck. On the floor, and he went under the truck. They thought he's dead. They left him. They didn't touch him anymore. At night, he went another group, which he wasn't registered in that group. I went home. I came home. And I said to my-- my daughter didn't understand yet. I said to myself, that's it. Finished. The chapter is finished. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] At night, you know, the neighbors came already. They came already to pay me a shiva call. At night, a truck stops by the window. I didn't even go near it because I know what-- I have no interest anymore. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Somebody is knocking on the door. The little one started to jump in crib and screaming, [NON-ENGLISH]. I said, yeah, yeah. You wait and see. And sure enough, the door opened and he walked in. I went in such a hysterical-- not in crying, no, in laughing, that they had to call help to revive me. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So this is one war to the other.

speaker: interviewer
text: After he was no longer fighting, what kind of work did he get?

speaker: interviewee
text: His brother send him-- like here is the Yellow Caps. He sent him from here from America. He had two brothers in America. He had a sister in Russia, and he had a rabbi brother in Russia. And he, when we came to [NON-ENGLISH], after a while he found somebody give him a letter from his sister. The brother didn't know from the sister and the sister didn't know from the brother. So he got them together, and his brothers in the states [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] came before the war. And one of his brothers were very well off-- very well off. So he came to visit us in Israel, and my husband got sick with ulcers. He was very, very sick. That time, they didn't operate in Israel yet. It was too serious. So his brother insists that he should come to America. He came to America, and he didn't want to go alone to be operated. And so they decided that I should come. I came, and I never went back, and I'm sorry.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when did-- when did you come to the United States?

speaker: interviewee
text: '57. He came in '56, because he said he will be operated, and he'll come back to Israel. When I got the first letter from him, what this-- that maybe it's-- he's suggesting, maybe, maybe, I should think about and see.

speaker: interviewee
text: I said, the kids were born in Israel. Two kids, my son and my daughter, in Israel. I'll take them away from there and bring them to America? No way. He started to write and write, and beg me and beg me. And the kids were very sick. They missed him very much.

speaker: interviewee
text: My daughter was in and out in the hospital when he left. They're just nervous cramps in her stomach, and they couldn't find out what-- what's the matter.

speaker: interviewer
text: And here, when he-- when he recovered, what did he do?

speaker: interviewee
text: He didn't op-- until I came, he didn't go under the knife. '57 he was operated.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when he recovered?

speaker: interviewee
text: And then he worked for my brother-in-law, for his brother that had the factory, and he was in charge of-- I mean, it was-- the shipping was belonged to him. He put trucks-- he helped him to put trucks and it wasn't easy.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what were you doing?

speaker: interviewee
text: That time, I had three small kids.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were home?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was home. But after that, when the kids started to go to school, I went with him to work. Believe me, I wasn't a lady of leisure. Never.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did your children go to school?

speaker: interviewee
text: When we came here, they went to [NON-ENGLISH]. My daughter finished, and then she went to public-- to [INAUDIBLE]. My son went about a year and a half, two years, and he couldn't take it, eight hours. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] And my little one that was born here [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] went to public school. And in the afternoon, three times a week, he went to the [INAUDIBLE]. They all had bar mitzvahs. They all had weddings. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] That's it.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you think kept you going throughout the whole experience?

speaker: interviewee
text: My husband's [INAUDIBLE]. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] He was very strong and a very big believer.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is there anything that you--

speaker: interviewee
text: It's not even a quarter. It's just, you know--

speaker: interviewer
text: Is there anything that you taught your children, based on your experience?

speaker: interviewee
text: My children live on my experience. They'll do anything, especially my middle one. There is not a thing that he wouldn't do to memorize his-- to-- to memorize his father in-- in different ways. There is not a Holocaust thing, there is not a thing they should sometimes say, or refuse, or not come, or-- or not be interested or-- or, you know. They are-- in my opinion, they are raised in our background. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And my husband would be very happy they would know. Because in the beginning, we kept quiet. That's why they are complaining all the time. If daddy wanted to say something, I thought it will destroy them. I was so anxious for family. I was saw anxious for to have somebody. All alone in my life. So I just didn't want to destroy them. But my head didn't understand where I should maybe use this stupid head.

speaker: interviewee
text: Did not understand that, if I-- if they would know, maybe I could share. Maybe I could spill. And I was afraid to scare them, to frighten them. But which they cannot forgive me even today. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Why didn't you talk? Why didn't you tell us?

speaker: interviewee
text: But I told that I'm stupid. By not telling them, now I think so. They think that they knew and understood all the time-- somebody's knocking on the door-- that something-- something is going on. That something that we're not telling them. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And they knew, if you'll talk to them, you'll see their opinions.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, I'd like to stop now. Thank you for coming this far with me.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you for listening to me.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I'd like to film the photographs. And then when they come, we'll-- we'll set up for a family shot. And I'll ask you to-- to make one statement. I'll ask-- I think they want to say something also.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh yeah. Definitely. And I would appreciate if they would say.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I would like you-- the question that I'll ask you is, what is your message for future generations. You'll be there with the future generations.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right. My answer is a very clear one.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. You-- you'll give it to us then. OK. Can you tell us who's in this photograph?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. On my right is my grandfather, Yudel Ticktin. And on the left is my aunt, his daughter, Shana Lea.

speaker: interviewer
text: And do you know approximately when the photograph was taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I don't remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was taken at-- in Bialystok or in [INAUDIBLE]. I don't know. I found them here in the United States.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. How did you get all of these pre-war photographs?

speaker: interviewee
text: My aunt kept it for me here, that she had them with her when she came 70 years or 80 years ago to America.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was this picture taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was taken in [INAUDIBLE], right near my-- the liquor store of my grandfather in 1927. There is me, nine months old, my grandfather, my aunt, Rifka, my grandmother, Liba, my father, my aunt, my brother, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] a orphan that was-- that was raised in my grandfather's house, and my aunt's husband.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell us where your father is.

speaker: interviewee
text: My father is right on the left, sitting.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where is your mother?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother is holding me on her lap, on the right.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, and the-- your grandfather?

speaker: interviewee
text: My grandfather's all the way on the right, in the corner.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the other people, can you tell us where they are in the photograph?

speaker: interviewee
text: My aunt, Rifka, is sitting right near my grandfather. My aunt, Shana Lea is sitting right near my father. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And my grandma is sitting between Rifka and her other daughter. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And my brother's right in the front with the bandage on his knee. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And that's my uncle, my aunt Shana Lea's husband.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where is he?

speaker: interviewee
text: On the right. [INAUDIBLE] On the right.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Is there anyone else who we need to identify there?

speaker: interviewee
text: And right near my mother is sitting my-- is my aunt, the one that gave me all these pictures.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. What is her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Evelyn.

speaker: interviewer
text: Stand.

speaker: interviewee
text: Standing on the left is my father, Alter Ticktin. Next to him is my mother, Zina Ticktin.

speaker: interviewer
text: And do you know anyone else in the picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I don't know the rest of the family. I don't remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you have any idea when this photograph was taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it before you were born?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think so. It looks to me that it's the wedding dress, my mother's.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could be. And when would that have been, approximately?

speaker: interviewee
text: No idea. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I have no idea.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] OK. Who's in this photograph?

speaker: interviewee
text: Standing on the chair, it's me. Next to me is my brother, six years older than me.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what is your brother's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mayer.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how old were you in this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think two.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. And where was this taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was taken in [? Hardok, ?] probably. I have no-- I don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember taking the picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.
